## PART 1: THE DISCOVERY MINDSET

### 1.1 What Discovery Is — and Isn't

Discovery is not a pitch. Discovery is not a demo. Discovery is not a presentation about TUF's eight collections and seven quality standards.

**Discovery is the process of understanding a coach's program so deeply that when you eventually present a solution, it feels inevitable — not like a sale.**

A great discovery call accomplishes four things:

1. **You understand the program** — size, levels, sports, culture, current state
2. **The coach feels understood** — probably for the first time by a vendor
3. **You've uncovered pain** — the specific frustrations that create urgency for change
4. **You've built enough trust** — that the coach wants to see what you have

### 1.2 The Discovery Call Rules

**Rule 1: The First 10 Minutes Are Zero TUF, All Program**

Most vendors start discovery calls by talking about themselves. "We're TUF Sports. We make custom uniforms. We've been in business for..." 

TUF starts by asking about the coach's program. And keeps asking. For the first 10 minutes, the only TUF content is your name and your genuine curiosity about their program.

**Rule 2: Listen More Than You Talk**

The ratio should be 80% coach, 20% rep for the first 15 minutes. If you're talking more than the coach, you're pitching — not discovering. Shut up and listen.

**Rule 3: Don't Interrupt a Pain Point**

When a coach starts venting about their current vendor, let them. Don't jump in with solutions. Don't say "that's exactly why we built TUF." Just let them talk. The more they articulate their frustration, the more they sell themselves on change.

**Rule 4: Follow Emotion, Not Just Facts**

"12 kids on varsity" is a fact. "I'm tired of watching my seniors wear boxy jerseys in their last team photo" is emotion. Facts tell you what. Emotion tells you why it matters. Discovery follows both.

**Rule 5: The Coach Should Do 70% of the Talking**

If you end a 20-minute discovery call and you did most of the talking, you failed at discovery. The coach should walk away thinking: "That rep really listened." Not: "That rep really pitched."

### 1.3 The Discovery Call Structure

```
MINUTE 0–2      GREETING + RAPPORT
                "Coach, thanks for the time. I know you're busy. I was looking 
                at [School]'s [sport] program and I had some questions."

MINUTE 2–5      SITUATION QUESTIONS
                Program size, levels, current setup. Broad. Factual. Safe.

MINUTE 5–10     PROBLEM QUESTIONS
                What's not working? What would you change? Where's the 
                frustration? This is where pain surfaces.

MINUTE 10–13    IMPLICATION QUESTIONS
                What happens if the problem continues? What's the cost of 
                doing nothing? This creates urgency.

MINUTE 13–15    NEED-PAYOFF QUESTIONS
                What if the problem were solved? What would that mean for your 
                program? This builds desire for the solution.

MINUTE 15–20    THE ENERGY SHIFT
                Coach has signaled interest → Deploy the 4-lane moat. 
                Introduce PRO CUT. Transition to demo.
```

---

## PART 2: THE SPIN FRAMEWORK — ADAPTED FOR TUF

### 2.1 What SPIN Is

SPIN selling (Situation, Problem, Implication, Need-Payoff) is one of the most researched and validated sales methodologies in history. Developed by Neil Rackham through analysis of 35,000 sales calls, it identifies the question patterns that separate top performers from average performers.

For TUF, we adapt SPIN specifically for the high school athletic apparel context.

### 2.2 The Four Question Types

| Question Type | Purpose | Example | When to Use |
|---------------|---------|---------|-------------|
| **SITUATION** | Understand the coach's current reality | "How many kids are in your program?" | Early — first 5 minutes |
| **PROBLEM** | Uncover dissatisfaction and pain points | "What's your biggest frustration with your current uniforms?" | Minutes 5–10 |
| **IMPLICATION** | Make the pain real — explore consequences | "What happens if uniforms arrive late for the season opener?" | Minutes 10–13 |
| **NEED-PAYOFF** | Help the coach articulate the value of a solution | "If the fit was sharp and delivery was guaranteed, what would that mean for your program?" | Minutes 13–15 |

### 2.3 SPIN Dos and Don'ts

| Do | Don't |
|----|-------|
| ✅ Ask open-ended questions | ❌ Ask leading questions that point to TUF features |
| ✅ Let silence sit — the coach will fill it | ❌ Jump in with solutions at the first sign of pain |
| ✅ Follow the coach's emotional cues | ❌ Stick rigidly to your question list |
| ✅ Use "tell me more" when they reveal pain | ❌ Say "we can fix that" before understanding the full problem |
| ✅ Sequence: S → P → I → N (generally) | ❌ Skip to Need-Payoff before establishing real problems |

---

## PART 3: SITUATION QUESTIONS BANK (20+ Questions)

Situation questions establish the facts. They're safe, broad, and non-threatening. They show the coach you're interested in understanding their world — not just selling into it.

### 3.1 Program Overview Questions

| # | Question | What You're Listening For |
|---|----------|--------------------------|
| 1 | "Coach, tell me about your program. How many kids? What levels are you running?" | Program size (small = budget sensitive; large = multi-lane opportunity). Levels (JV, freshman, youth = expansion opportunities). |
| 2 | "How many sports does [School] offer total? Which are the biggest?" | Multi-sport opportunity. Which sports have the most athletes/parents/boosters. |
| 3 | "What's the culture of your program? How would you describe it to someone who's never seen you play?" | Coach's identity. Pride points. What they value beyond wins/losses. |
| 4 | "How long have you been coaching at [School]? What drew you here?" | Relationship building. Long-tenured coaches have deep community ties. New coaches may have authority to change vendors. |
| 5 | "Tell me about your coaching staff. How many assistants? How long have they been with you?" | Program stability. Large staff = more mouths to satisfy. Long-tenured staff = established systems. |

### 3.2 Current Uniform Questions

| # | Question | What You're Listening For |
|---|----------|--------------------------|
| 6 | "What are you wearing now? Who's your current uniform provider?" | Brand name (Nike, Adidas, BSN, local). This tells you the competitive landscape. |
| 7 | "How long have you been with [current vendor]?" | Inertia risk. 10+ years = strong relationship or deep inertia. |
| 8 | "Walk me through your uniform setup. Home, away, alternates? What levels get new uniforms and how often?" | Budget allocation. Which levels are prioritized. Rotation frequency. |
| 9 | "What's your uniform budget look like? Is it district-funded, booster-funded, or a combination?" | Budget source. This determines who you need to sell to (AD, booster club, or both). |
| 10 | "How many uniforms do you typically order per season? For how many athletes?" | Order volume. Determines revenue opportunity. |

### 3.3 Ordering Process Questions

| # | Question | What You're Listening For |
|---|----------|--------------------------|
| 11 | "Walk me through your ordering process. Who's involved? What's the timeline?" | Decision-maker identification. Process friction points. |
| 12 | "When do you typically place your uniform order for [season]?" | Ordering window. Timing for proposal delivery. |
| 13 | "Who makes the final decision on uniform vendors — is it you, the AD, the booster club, or someone else?" | Decision-maker mapping. This is critical — selling the wrong person wastes time. |
| 14 | "Does the district have any vendor contracts or preferred supplier lists you have to work within?" | Contract lock-in. If there's a district contract, you need to know what it covers. |

### 3.4 Program Identity Questions

| # | Question | What You're Listening For |
|---|----------|--------------------------|
| 15 | "When your team walks out on game day, how do you want them to look? How do you want them to feel?" | Emotional trigger. The coach's vision. This is the "why" behind the purchase. |
| 16 | "What do your athletes care about most in their uniforms — fit, look, comfort, something else?" | Athlete perspective. What will make the kids excited about TUF. |
| 17 | "What do the parents and boosters care about? What do they notice?" | Stakeholder priorities. Parents care about cost and durability. Boosters care about how the program looks. |
| 18 | "What are your school colors? Mascot? Is there a specific look or tradition you want to maintain?" | Design constraints. Some schools have strong traditions around specific uniform elements. |

### 3.5 Facility and Logistics Questions

| # | Question | What You're Listening For |
|---|----------|--------------------------|
| 19 | "How do you handle sizing? Do you do it yourself, or does your current vendor help?" | Sizing pain point. If the coach does it themselves, they'll appreciate TUF's sizing sessions. |
| 20 | "How's the storage situation? Do you have enough uniforms for all levels, or is it tight?" | Inventory pain. Programs that are short on uniforms have expansion opportunity. |
| 21 | "What else is on your wish list — beyond uniforms? Travel bags? Warmups? Spirit wear?" | Lane expansion opportunity. Listen for mentions of travel gear, fan gear, jackets. |
| 22 | "Do you have a team store or spirit wear program? How's that working?" | Lane 3 opportunity. If they have one, what's working? If not, why not? |

---

## PART 4: PROBLEM QUESTIONS BANK (20+ Questions)

Problem questions uncover dissatisfaction. This is where you move from facts to frustration. The goal is to help the coach articulate problems they may have normalized or accepted as inevitable.

### 4.1 Fit and Quality Problems

| # | Question | What You're Listening For |
|---|----------|--------------------------|
| 1 | "What's your biggest frustration with your current uniforms?" | Open-ended invitation to vent. Let them lead. Follow wherever they go. |
| 2 | "How's the fit on your current uniforms? Are your athletes happy with how they look and feel?" | PRO CUT opportunity. "Boxy" is the magic word. "They're fine" = settling. |
| 3 | "Have you ever looked at a team photo and thought 'those jerseys don't look right'? What was wrong?" | Visual pain point. The team photo is the permanent record. Boxy jerseys in photos are irreversible evidence of a bad fit. |
| 4 | "How do the uniforms hold up over a season? Any issues with stitching, fading, graphics peeling?" | LOCK-FIT and sublimation opportunity. Listen for specific failure points. |
| 5 | "How does the fabric feel during games — especially late in the season? Does it breathe? Stay light?" | AIR-FORGE and POWER-STRETCH opportunity. Heavy, sweat-soaked jerseys are a universal complaint. |

### 4.2 Vendor Relationship Problems

| # | Question | What You're Listening For |
|---|----------|--------------------------|
| 6 | "How's your relationship with your current rep? Do you feel like a priority?" | Relationship pain. "I can never reach him" = IRONCLAD GUARANTEE response. "He's fine" = inertia. |
| 7 | "When something goes wrong with an order — wrong size, missing item — what happens? How's it handled?" | Service pain. Long resolution times, finger-pointing, "it's not our fault" = IRONCLAD GUARANTEE opportunity. |
| 8 | "Have you ever had a uniform order arrive late? What was that like?" | Delivery pain. Late uniforms = angry parents, embarrassed athletes, stressed coach. |
| 9 | "Does your current vendor proactively reach out, or are you always the one chasing them?" | Proactive vs. reactive service. TUF's stay-warm cadence and check-in philosophy is different. |

### 4.3 Process and Logistics Problems

| # | Question | What You're Listening For |
|---|----------|--------------------------|
| 10 | "What's the most painful part of the uniform ordering process for you?" | Process pain. Sizing headaches, artwork delays, budget approval, last-minute changes. |
| 11 | "How do you handle uniforms for the lower levels — JV, freshman? Same quality or different?" | Level inequality. If varsity gets premium and JV gets cheap, there's an opportunity to standardize. |
| 12 | "Do you have enough uniforms for all your athletes, or are you making it work with what you've got?" | Inventory shortage. Programs making do are prime for expansion. |
| 13 | "How many different vendors are you juggling right now — for uniforms, bags, spirit wear, jackets?" | Vendor fragmentation. The 4-lane moat directly addresses this pain. |

### 4.4 Sport-Specific Problems

| # | Question | Sport | What You're Listening For |
|---|----------|-------|--------------------------|
| 14 | "How do your football uniforms hold up through contact? Any tearing at stress points?" | Football | LOCK-FIT opportunity. Contact sports expose construction weaknesses. |
| 15 | "How's the waistband on your basketball shorts? Any bunching, twisting — especially on the reversible ones?" | Basketball | FLEX-WAIST opportunity. This is the #1 basketball pain point. Women's coaches react strongest. |
| 16 | "Do your baseball/softball pants hold up to sliding? How are the knees after a season?" | Baseball/Softball | Durability opportunity. Reinforced knees, quality fabric. |
| 17 | "How do your uniforms perform in heat? August two-a-days — do your kids feel like they're wearing plastic bags?" | Football | AIR-FORGE opportunity. Heat management is a real concern. |
| 18 | "How do your volleyball uniforms fit your athletes? Any complaints about the shorts riding up or jerseys being too boxy?" | Volleyball | PRO CUT + fit opportunity. Volleyball requires specific fit considerations. |

### 4.5 The Deep Pain Questions

| # | Question | Purpose |
|---|----------|---------|
| 19 | "If you could change one thing about your current uniform situation — with a magic wand — what would it be?" | Uncovers the deepest frustration. The "magic wand" framing gives permission to wish. |
| 20 | "What's a uniform problem you've just learned to live with — something that bothers you but you've accepted because you think it's just how it is?" | Surfaces normalized pain. The thing they don't even think about as solvable. |
| 21 | "Be honest — if your athletes could choose their uniforms, would they pick what you're wearing now?" | Athlete perspective. Kids are honest. If the athletes don't like them, that's leverage. |

---

## PART 5: IMPLICATION QUESTIONS BANK

Implication questions make the pain real. They explore the consequences of unsolved problems. They create urgency. Without implication, a coach might acknowledge a problem but lack motivation to fix it.

### 5.1 The Purpose of Implication

A coach saying "the jerseys are a little boxy" is a mild complaint. A coach realizing "those boxy jerseys are in every team photo, every newspaper article, every parent's Facebook post — that's the permanent record of our program" is motivation to change.

Implication questions take a problem from "that's annoying" to "that's unacceptable."

### 5.2 Implication Questions

| # | Problem Identified | Implication Question |
|---|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Boxy fit | "When you look at your team photo from last season — the one that hangs in the school hallway — are you proud of how your athletes look? Or do you notice the fit issues?" |
| 2 | Peeling graphics | "What happens if graphics start peeling mid-season? Do parents complain? Do your athletes feel self-conscious?" |
| 3 | Late delivery | "If uniforms arrive a week before the first game, what does that do to your preparation? Your athletes' confidence?" |
| 4 | Wrong sizes | "What happens when a senior gets a jersey that doesn't fit and there's no time to reorder? What does that feel like — for them and for you?" |
| 5 | Can't reach rep | "When you've got a problem and your rep is unreachable — who deals with the fallout? The AD? The parents? You?" |
| 6 | Vendor fragmentation | "You're managing four different vendors right now — uniforms, bags, spirit wear, jackets. How much of your time goes to vendor management instead of coaching?" |
| 7 | Poor durability | "If jerseys are falling apart by playoffs, what message does that send to your athletes about how much their program matters?" |
| 8 | Budget constraints | "If the budget means you're choosing between quality and quantity — what's the hidden cost of cheap uniforms you replace every year versus quality uniforms that last three seasons?" |
| 9 | Unequal levels | "If your JV kids are wearing hand-me-downs while varsity gets new gear — what does that say about how you value those athletes?" |
| 10 | Bunching waistbands (basketball) | "Your players are folding their waistbands before every game because they bunch up. How many times have you seen a kid adjust their shorts during a crucial possession?" |
| 11 | Heavy/sweaty fabric | "Fourth quarter, game on the line, and your kids are wearing jerseys that weigh 10 pounds from sweat. What does that do to their performance?" |
| 12 | Generic look | "When you line up against [rival school] and they look sharp and your team looks... fine — what does that do to your kids' mindset before the game even starts?" |
| 13 | No team store | "Your community wants to support the program, but they're wearing generic school T-shirts. What would it mean if they could wear gear that actually looks like your program?" |
| 14 | No letterman jackets | "Your seniors have given four years to this program. When they graduate, what do they have to show for it — physically, tangibly?" |
| 15 | Missed budget window | "If you miss this budget cycle, that's another full season in uniforms you're not happy with. What does another year of settling cost your program?" |

### 5.3 Implication Follow-Up Phrases

When the coach answers an implication question, follow up with:

- "Tell me more about that."
- "What was that like?"
- "How did that affect [the athletes / the parents / your preparation]?"
- "Has that happened more than once?"
- "What did you do about it?"

---

## PART 6: NEED-PAYOFF QUESTIONS BANK

Need-payoff questions help the coach articulate the value of solving their problems. Instead of you telling them why TUF is great, they tell themselves.

### 6.1 The Purpose of Need-Payoff

Need-payoff questions shift the conversation from "here's what's wrong" to "here's what could be right." They create desire. They make the coach picture the solution — and in doing so, they sell themselves.

The best need-payoff questions don't mention TUF at all. They ask the coach to describe what the ideal would look like. The implication: TUF can deliver that ideal.

### 6.2 Need-Payoff Questions

| # | Context | Need-Payoff Question |
|---|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | After discussing boxy fit | "If your athletes walked out in uniforms that fit like what college players wear — sharp taper, articulated sleeves — what would that do for their confidence?" |
| 2 | After discussing vendor unreliability | "Imagine you had a vendor where one call solved any problem — and problems got fixed, period. What would it mean to not have to worry about uniforms?" |
| 3 | After discussing vendor fragmentation | "What if you had one vendor for uniforms, travel gear, spirit wear, and letterman jackets — one relationship, one call — how would that change your life as a coach?" |
| 4 | After discussing late deliveries | "If you knew — guaranteed — that your uniforms would be in hand two weeks before the first game, every season, what would that do for your peace of mind?" |
| 5 | After discussing durability issues | "What if your uniforms looked as sharp in the playoffs as they did on Day 1 — and you didn't have to think about replacements for 3 seasons?" |
| 6 | After discussing athlete complaints | "If your athletes put on a uniform and their first reaction was 'this feels different' — in a good way — what would that say about your program?" |
| 7 | After discussing budget concerns | "What if the uniform investment could partially pay for itself — through a team store where your program earns revenue on every sale?" |
| 8 | After discussing hand-me-down culture | "What if every level — varsity, JV, freshman — wore uniforms built to the same quality standard? What would that communicate?" |
| 9 | After discussing waistband problems (basketball) | "If your players never had to think about their waistbands during a game — just played — what would that free up mentally?" |
| 10 | After discussing travel gear | "When your team walks through an airport or into an away gym, and they're wearing a coordinated travel system — not random bags — what does that say about who you are as a program?" |
| 11 | After discussing fan/community engagement | "What if your community — parents, students, alumni — could buy gear that actually looks like your program, and every purchase funded the program?" |
| 12 | After discussing senior recognition | "When your seniors walk across the stage at the end-of-year banquet, what should they be wearing that carries four years of memories?" |

### 6.3 The Transition: Need-Payoff to TUF

After the coach has described their ideal (through your need-payoff questions), the transition to TUF sounds like this:

> *"Coach, you just described exactly what we built TUF to deliver. Not because we guessed what programs need — but because our founder was a coach who wanted the same things you just described. Let me show you what that looks like."*

This is the energy shift — from discovery to demo. The coach has painted the picture. Now you show them TUF is the brush.

---

## PART 7: BUDGET DISCOVERY

### 7.1 Why Budget Discovery Matters

Price is rarely the real objection — but it's always the stated objection. Understanding a coach's budget situation before you present pricing prevents the "we can't afford it" stall and allows you to frame the investment properly.

### 7.2 Budget Discovery Questions

| # | Question | What You're Learning |
|---|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | "How does uniform funding work in your district? Is it allocated by the school board, raised by boosters, or a mix?" | Budget source. District funding = bureaucratic process. Booster funding = relationship and presentation matters. |
| 2 | "What's the ballpark you're working with for uniforms this season?" | Budget range. This tells you which tier to propose. |
| 3 | "What are you paying per uniform right now?" | Current spend. This is your comparison anchor. |
| 4 | "Has your uniform budget changed in the last few years? Up, down, flat?" | Budget trend. Shrinking budgets = focus on value and durability. Growing budgets = opportunity for premium tiers. |
| 5 | "When does the budget get finalized for next season? Who needs to approve it?" | Budget timeline. If the budget is already locked, you're selling for next cycle. If it's open, urgency is real. |
| 6 | "Do your boosters contribute to uniforms? How active is the booster club?" | Booster involvement. Active boosters can supplement district budgets. |

### 7.3 How to Discuss Pricing Without Scaring Coaches

**The Pricing Conversation Rule:** Never discuss specific pricing until the coach has seen the value.

| Timing | What to Say |
|--------|------------|
| If coach asks price early (before demo) | "Coach, I want to make sure I give you an accurate number based on what your program actually needs — not a generic price. Let me learn a little more about your program first, and then I'll put together something specific." |
| If coach presses on price | "I understand budget is real. Let me show you what you're getting for the investment — PRO CUT fit, [sport standard], IRONCLAD GUARANTEE — and then we'll talk numbers. Fair?" |
| If coach says "just give me a ballpark" | "For a program your size, most schools invest between $X and $Y for a full uniform set. But that depends on exactly what you need. Let me put together something specific for [School]." |

### 7.4 The Value Frame

When you do discuss pricing, frame it as investment, not expense:

| Expense Frame | Investment Frame |
|---------------|-----------------|
| "This jersey costs $85." | "Your athletes will wear this uniform for 3 seasons — that's about $28 per season." |
| "The full set is $5,000." | "That's 25 athletes, 2 uniforms each, across varsity and JV — with the IRONCLAD GUARANTEE that we fix anything." |
| "We're more expensive than your current vendor." | "The stock blank gets replaced next year. The TUF uniform is still sharp in Year 3. Different math." |

---

## PART 8: DECISION-MAKER IDENTIFICATION

### 8.1 Who Actually Signs the Check?

One of the most common mistakes in sales is selling to the wrong person. You might have a great relationship with the head football coach — but if the AD controls the budget and has a 15-year relationship with the BSN rep, you're selling to the wrong person.

### 8.2 The Decision-Maker Map

| Role | Typical Authority | How to Engage |
|------|------------------|---------------|
| **Head Coach** | Recommends. Strong influence on uniform choice. Often controls sport-specific budget. | Primary relationship. They're the user and often the champion. |
| **Athletic Director** | Approves. Controls athletic department budget. Often has vendor relationships. | Must engage for any significant deal. The closer for district-funded programs. |
| **Booster Club President** | Funds. Raises and allocates money. Often controls supplemental budget. | Critical for programs where boosters fund uniforms. Present the value and the team store model. |
| **Principal** | Signs off on large expenditures. Usually rubber-stamps AD recommendations. | Rarely the selling target. AD handles the recommendation. |
| **District Office / School Board** | Approves major contracts. District-wide vendor agreements. | Only relevant for large, district-level deals. |
| **Assistant Coach** | Influences. May handle logistics. Can be an internal champion. | Don't ignore them. They often handle the details and can advocate for you. |

### 8.3 Decision-Maker Discovery Questions

| # | Question |
|---|----------|
| 1 | "Who makes the final call on uniforms — is it you, or does the AD need to sign off?" |
| 2 | "Walk me through the approval process. You decide you want to switch — what happens next?" |
| 3 | "Who else needs to be in the room when we discuss this? Is there anyone whose opinion carries a lot of weight?" |
| 4 | "Does the booster club have a say in uniform decisions? Are they involved in funding?" |
| 5 | "Has anyone else at the school expressed frustration with the current uniforms? The AD? Other coaches?" |

### 8.4 Multi-Stakeholder Strategy

If multiple people are involved in the decision:

1. **Identify the champion.** Who wants change the most? Empower them to advocate internally.
2. **Arm the champion.** Give them talking points, a sample, a one-page summary they can share.
3. **Offer to present to the group.** "Coach, if it would help, I'd be happy to do a 15-minute presentation for you and the AD — show the product, explain the guarantee, and answer questions directly."
4. **Don't go around your champion.** If the coach says the AD decides, ask the coach for an introduction — don't cold-call the AD behind the coach's back.

---

## PART 9: TIMELINE MAPPING

### 9.1 Understanding the School's Purchasing Calendar

Every school has a rhythm. Understanding that rhythm tells you when to push, when to wait, and when the opportunity window closes.

### 9.2 The School Budget Calendar

| Period | What's Happening | Your Action |
|--------|-----------------|-------------|
| **JAN–FEB** | AD begins budget planning for next school year | "Coach, let's get TUF numbers in front of you before the budget locks." |
| **MAR–APR** | Budget proposals submitted to district | Proposals must be in hand. Coaches need pricing to include in budget requests. |
| **MAY–JUN** | Budget approved by school board | Follow up: "Did the uniform budget get approved? Let's get the order in." |
| **JUL 1** | New fiscal year begins | Budget available. Fall sport orders finalize. |
| **SEP–OCT** | Mid-year adjustments (some districts) | Supplemental budget windows — opportunity for programs that missed spring. |

### 9.3 Sport-Specific Ordering Windows

| Sport Season | Ordering Window | Urgency Trigger |
|-------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| **Fall (Football, Volleyball, Soccer, XC)** | MAR–APR (primary), MAY (secondary) | Spring practice. "Coach, let's lock in GRIND before spring ball." |
| **Winter (Basketball, Hockey, Wrestling)** | JUL–AUG (primary), SEP–OCT (secondary) | Summer league/camps. "Coach, your kids are playing summer ball. Let's get OVERTIME locked in." |
| **Spring (Baseball, Softball, Track, Golf)** | OCT–NOV (primary), DEC–JAN (secondary) | Fall ball. "Coach, your kids are playing fall ball. Let's lock in DIAMOND for spring." |

### 9.4 Timeline Discovery Questions

| # | Question |
|---|----------|
| 1 | "When does your ordering cycle typically start for [season]?" |
| 2 | "When do you need uniforms in hand? Is there a hard deadline — first game, media day, something else?" |
| 3 | "When does the budget get locked in for next season? Is there still time to get a proposal in?" |
| 4 | "What's your lead time been with your current vendor? How far in advance do you typically order?" |
| 5 | "Is there a booster club meeting or AD review coming up where uniform decisions are discussed?" |

---

## PART 10: MULTI-SPORT DISCOVERY

### 10.1 The Opportunity Beyond the Initial Sport

You might be talking to a football coach. But that school also has basketball, baseball, softball, volleyball, soccer, track, and more. Every sport is a potential lane. Every coach is a potential champion in a different part of the athletic department.

### 10.2 Multi-Sport Discovery Questions

| # | Question | Opportunity |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | "What other sports are big at [School]? Is there another program that could benefit from what we're talking about?" | Opens the door to other sports. The coach often knows which programs are frustrated with their uniforms. |
| 2 | "Does the basketball program use the same vendor as football, or do they do their own thing?" | Two-sport opportunity. Different sports often have different vendors — fragmentation you can solve. |
| 3 | "Is there a girls' sport that's been asking for better uniforms? Volleyball? Basketball? Softball?" | Women's Division opportunity. Girls' programs are often under-resourced. FLEX-WAIST is a powerful differentiator for girls' basketball. |
| 4 | "Do the lower levels — JV, freshman, middle school — get new uniforms, or is it hand-me-downs?" | Volume opportunity. If only varsity gets new uniforms, there's room to expand to all levels with consistent quality. |
| 5 | "When your athletes travel, what are they wearing? Do you have coordinated travel gear, or is it whatever they bring?" | Lane 2 (ISSUE travel gear) opportunity. Travel gear is the fastest cross-sell. |
| 6 | "Does the school have a team store for spirit wear? Do parents and fans have a way to buy program gear?" | Lane 3 (Team Stores) opportunity. The always-open lane. |
| 7 | "What about letterman jackets? Who handles those — the school, the boosters, individual families?" | Lane 4 (LEGACY) opportunity. Usually handled separately — prime for consolidation. |

### 10.3 The Multi-Sport Handoff

If the football coach mentions the basketball program could use help:

> *"Coach, I'd love to connect with the basketball coach. Would you be comfortable making an introduction? Or if you prefer, I can reach out directly and mention we've been working with your football program. Either way — I want to make sure the whole athletic department benefits, not just one sport."*

---

---

## PART 12: THE DISCOVERY TO DEMO TRANSITION

### 12.1 When to Transition

You've asked your Situation questions. You've uncovered Problems. You've explored Implications. The coach has described what they need through Need-Payoff questions. Now — how do you know it's time to introduce TUF?

**Signals the coach is ready:**

- They've mentioned a specific frustration you can solve
- They've asked "so what do you do differently?"
- They've expressed curiosity about TUF ("I've never heard of you — what's your story?")
- They've acknowledged that change might be worth considering
- Their tone has shifted from defensive to open

**Signals the coach is NOT ready:**

- They're still giving short, guarded answers
- They haven't revealed any frustration
- They've said "we're happy with what we have" and meant it
- They're distracted or clearly trying to get off the phone

### 12.2 The Energy Shift Language

When it's time to introduce TUF after discovery, use language that connects what the coach just told you to what TUF delivers:

> *"Coach, you just told me three things: [their pain point 1], [pain point 2], and you wish you had [their need-payoff]. Here's why I think we should talk further: TUF was built specifically to solve exactly what you're describing."*

Then deploy the 4-lane moat:

> *"We don't sell uniforms. We outfit programs. Four lanes — game day, travel, fans, tradition. One system. One look. One relationship. Nobody else thinks about your program that way."*

Then introduce the fit differentiator:

> *"And everything we build — PRO CUT fit to college specifications, LOCK-FIT construction, and the IRONCLAD GUARANTEE — is designed to solve the exact frustrations you just described."*

Then transition to the demo:

> *"Let me show you what this actually looks like. I've got a sample right here..."*

### 12.3 If the Coach Isn't Ready

If the coach hasn't shown buying signals, don't force the transition. Instead:

> *"Coach, I've learned a lot about your program today — thank you for that. I don't want to pitch you on something if the timing isn't right. Let me leave you with my card and a couple things to think about. If anything changes with your current situation, I'm here. No pressure."*

Then leave a sample. The sample is the Trojan horse. It sells when you're not in the room.

---

## PART 13: 20 ROLE-PLAY DISCOVERY SCENARIOS

### Instructions

Practice with your Director or a fellow rep. One plays Rep, one plays Coach. The Rep's goal is to execute a full discovery conversation — Situation → Problem → Implication → Need-Payoff → Energy Shift. After each scenario, debrief.

---

### Scenario 1: The Satisfied Coach (Difficult)

**Coach Profile:** Head Football Coach. Tier 2 suburban school. Happy with Nike. No complaints. Genuinely satisfied.

> *"Honestly, we love Nike. The fit is good, delivery is reliable, kids love the swoosh. I don't see a reason to switch."*

**Objective:** Acknowledge satisfaction. Plant a seed about the 4-lane system. Leave a sample. Don't push.

**Model Discovery Flow:**
1. Situation: "Tell me about your program. How many kids, what levels?"
2. Problem: "So everything's working — fit, delivery, price. That's rare. One thing you'd change if you could?"
3. (Coach: "Honestly, nothing. It's good.")
4. Implication: Skip — no pain to amplify.
5. Need-Payoff: "What if one vendor could handle not just uniforms but travel gear, team stores, and letterman jackets — one system?"
6. Energy Shift: "Coach, I'm not here to break a good thing. Let me leave you a GRIND sample. If anything changes, I'm here."

---

### Scenario 2: The Frustrated Basketball Coach (Easy)

**Coach Profile:** Girls' Basketball Coach. Current vendor is a local screen printer. Uniforms are boxy. Waistbands bunch up. Graphics are peeling after one season.

> *"These uniforms are terrible. The girls hate them. The waistbands are a disaster — they're constantly folding them. And the graphics? Peeling after 3 washes."*

**Objective:** Let her vent. Connect each pain point to a TUF solution. Schedule a demo ASAP.

**Model Discovery Flow:**
1. Situation: "Tell me about your program, Coach."
2. Problem: "What's the biggest frustration?"
3. (Coach vents about waistbands.)
4. Implication: "How many times in a game does a player adjust her shorts? What does that do to her focus?"
5. Need-Payoff: "If the waistband stayed flat — no bunching, no folding — what would that mean?"
6. Energy Shift: "Coach, you just described the exact problem our FLEX-WAIST solves. Let me bring you OVERTIME samples this week."

---

### Scenario 3: The Time-Pressed AD

**Coach Profile:** Athletic Director. Overseeing 14 sports. 5-minute phone window. Direct. No patience for a long discovery process.

> *"I've got 5 minutes. What do I need to know?"*

**Objective:** Compress discovery into 5 minutes. Get the critical information. Schedule a longer follow-up.

**Model Discovery Flow:**
1. Situation (30 sec): "How many sports, Coach? What's your biggest uniform challenge across the department?"
2. Problem (1 min): "What's the most frustrating part of managing uniforms for 14 sports?"
3. Implication (1 min): "Managing four vendors — what's that costing you in time?"
4. Need-Payoff (30 sec): "One vendor, four lanes, one call if anything goes wrong — would that simplify things?"
5. Energy Shift (1 min): "Coach, that's exactly what we do. I can show you in 15 minutes. When's good this week?"

---

### Scenario 4: The "We Have No Money" Coach

**Coach Profile:** Head Softball Coach. Small rural school. Budget is near-zero. Current uniforms are 6+ years old.

> *"I'd love new uniforms. But we have zero budget. The booster club raises maybe $500 a year. We're barely keeping the lights on."*

**Objective:** Empathize. Introduce the team store model. Plant a seed. Don't sell.

**Model Discovery Flow:**
1. Situation: "Tell me about your program, Coach. How many girls?"
2. Problem: "What's the condition of the current uniforms?"
3. Implication: "How do the girls feel wearing 6-year-old uniforms when other teams in your conference have new gear?"
4. Need-Payoff: "What if there were a way to fund new uniforms without touching your school budget?"
5. Energy Shift: "Coach, let me tell you about our team store model. Your program earns 15–25% on every sale. Some programs fund their entire uniform order this way. I'll send you the info. No pressure. Just an option."

---

### Scenario 5: The Multi-Sport AD (Advanced)

**Coach Profile:** Athletic Director at large suburban school. 16 sports. Currently uses Nike for football/basketball, BSN for everything else, separate vendors for travel bags and letterman jackets. She's overwhelmed by the vendor management.

> *"Honestly, managing all these vendors is a part-time job I didn't sign up for. But I don't see how one company can do 16 sports well."*

**Objective:** Acknowledge the concern. Show the 4-lane system with sport-specific collections. Don't overpromise.

**Model Discovery Flow:**
1. Situation: "16 sports. That's a lot. Walk me through how you manage all the different vendors."
2. Problem: "What's the biggest headache of having 4+ vendor relationships?"
3. Implication: "How much of your week goes to vendor management instead of athletic department leadership?"
4. Need-Payoff: "What if you had one vendor — one relationship — with sport-specific expertise for each program?"
5. Energy Shift: "Coach, here's why we're different. We don't try to do 'everything.' We do four specific lanes — uniforms (GRIND for football, OVERTIME for basketball, DIAMOND for baseball/softball), travel gear (ISSUE), team stores, and letterman jackets (LEGACY). Each lane has sport-specific expertise. But they all share the same quality standard and one relationship. Start with one sport. If we deliver, expand."

---

### Scenario 6–20: Additional Scenarios (Summary)

| # | Scenario | Coach Type | Key Challenge |
|---|----------|-----------|---------------|
| 6 | New Coach | First-year head coach | Has authority to choose vendor. Needs everything. |
| 7 | Coach Near Retirement | 25-year veteran | Inertia. "I've done it this way for 25 years." |
| 8 | The Parent-Coach | Volunteer coach | Budget. "I'm just a parent trying to help." |
| 9 | The Angry Coach | Just had uniforms arrive 3 weeks late | Venting. Emotional. Ready to switch. |
| 10 | The "Call the AD" Coach | Interested but no authority | Must work through AD. Needs champion strategy. |
| 11 | Private School Coach | High expectations, competitive | Quality and prestige matter more than price. |
| 12 | Boys' Basketball Coach | AAU-connected | Used to premium gear. FLEX-WAIST differentiator. |
| 13 | Volleyball Coach | Underserved program | Often afterthought. PRO CUT fit + team store. |
| 14 | Hockey Coach | Minnesota hockey culture | Specific gear needs. Travel gear (ISSUE) emphasis. |
| 15 | Wrestling Coach | Tough, no-nonsense | Durability focus (LOCK-FIT). Lightweight singlets. |
| 16 | Soccer Coach | Club-to-school pipeline | Used to premium club gear. DIAMOND positioning. |
| 17 | Track/XC Coach | Large roster, low per-athlete budget | Value tier. CAMP collection. Team store. |
| 18 | The Booster Club President | Controls the money | Revenue-share model. Team store appeal. |
| 19 | The "Ghost" Coach | Interested, then disappears | Discovery got cut short. Follow-up strategy. |
| 20 | The Dream Coach | Ready to buy, big program, all sports | Don't get greedy. Start with one lane. Expand. |

**For each scenario, practice:**
1. Situation questions (2–3 min)
2. Problem questions (3–5 min)
3. Implication questions (2–3 min)
4. Need-Payoff questions (2–3 min)
5. Energy shift to TUF (2–3 min)

---

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    SPIN FOR TUF — FIELD CARD                     │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│  S — SITUATION (First 5 min):                                    │
│  "Tell me about your program."                                   │
│  "What are you wearing now?"                                     │
│  "Who makes the uniform decisions?"                              │
│  "What's your timeline?"                                         │
│                                                                  │
│  P — PROBLEM (Minutes 5–10):                                     │
│  "What's your biggest frustration with your current uniforms?"   │
│  "What would you change if you could?"                           │
│  "How's the fit? The delivery? The rep relationship?"            │
│                                                                  │
│  I — IMPLICATION (Minutes 10–13):                                │
│  "What happens when uniforms arrive late?"                       │
│  "How does [pain] affect your athletes' confidence?"             │
│  "What's the cost of another season with [problem]?"             │
│                                                                  │
│  N — NEED-PAYOFF (Minutes 13–15):                                │
│  "If [problem] were solved, what would that mean?"               │
│  "What would sharp fit + guaranteed delivery do for you?"        │
│  "Imagine one vendor for all four lanes — what changes?"         │
│                                                                  │
│  RULE: 80% coach talking. 20% rep talking.                      │
│  RULE: Don't pitch until the coach signals readiness.            │
│  RULE: Follow pain points — "Tell me more about that."           │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## APPENDIX B: PAIN-TO-SOLUTION MAPPING

| If Coach Says... | Pain Point | TUF Solution | Lead Standard |
|-----------------|-----------|-------------|---------------|
| "The jerseys look boxy" | Poor fit | PRO CUT — athletic-tapered, articulated sleeves | PRO CUT |
| "The waistbands bunch up" | Basketball reversible problem | FLEX-WAIST — engineered to stay flat | FLEX-WAIST |
| "They don't breathe" | Heat/sweat management | AIR-FORGE — thermal-mapped ventilation | AIR-FORGE |
| "The graphics peel" | Poor sublimation/decoration | Full sublimation — color bonded into fiber | (Show, don't name) |
| "They fall apart" | Poor construction | LOCK-FIT — triple-stitched, taped seams | LOCK-FIT |
| "Delivery is always late" | Unreliable vendor | IRONCLAD GUARANTEE + proactive timeline mgmt | IRONCLAD |
| "I can never reach my rep" | Poor service | One relationship. One call. Real person. | IRONCLAD |
| "I'm juggling 4 vendors" | Vendor fragmentation | 4-Lane system — one vendor, one system | 4-Lane Moat |
| "We can't afford quality" | Budget constraint | Team store revenue model. Multi-year durability. | Team Store (Lane 3) |
| "JV gets hand-me-downs" | Unequal levels | Same quality standard across all levels | PRO CUT |
| "Kids aren't proud of how they look" | Program identity | BUILT FOR THE PROGRAM — whole program identity | Brand concept |

---

*Discovery is where deals are won or lost. The rep who asks the best questions — and truly listens to the answers — builds trust that no competitor can break. Master discovery, and you master the TUF sales system.*

**BUILT FOR THE PROGRAM.**
